From Specification to Implementation

вҖў REs is only a language specification[еҸӘжҳҜе®ҡд№үдәҶиҜӯиЁҖ]
в—Ҷto construct a token recognizer for languages given by regular expressions
вҖў How do we go from specification to implementation?
в—ҶRegular expressions can be implemented using finite automata
в—ҶThere are two types of automata
пҒ°NFAs (nondeterministic finite automata) [йқһзЎ®е®ҡзҡ„жңүз©·иҮӘеҠЁжңә]
пҒ°DFAs (deterministic finite automata) [зЎ®е®ҡзҡ„жңүз©·иҮӘеҠЁжңә]
Finite Automata[жңүз©·иҮӘеҠЁжңә] 
4
Finite Automata(FA) [жңүз©·иҮӘеҠЁжңә] 
Regular
expressions
NFA
DFA
Table-driven 
Implementation
Lexical
Specification

вҖў Regular Expression = specification[жӯЈеҲҷиЎЁиҫҫжҳҜе®ҡд№ү]
вҖў Finite Automata = implementation[иҮӘеҠЁжңәжҳҜе®һзҺ°]
вҖў Automaton (pl. automata): a machine or program
вҖў Finite automaton (FA): a program with a finite number of states
вҖў Finite Automata are similar to transition diagrams
в—ҶThey have states and labelled edges
в—ҶThere are one unique start state and one or more than one final states
Finite Automata[жңүз©·иҮӘеҠЁжңә] 
5

вҖў Node[иҠӮзӮ№]: state
в—ҶEach state represents a condition that may occur in the process
в—ҶInitial state (Start): only one, circle marked with вҖҳstartвҖҷ 
в—ҶFinal state (Accepting): may have multiple, double circle
вҖў Edge[иҫ№]: transition.  directed, labeled with the symbol(s)
в—ҶFrom one state to another on the input
Transition Diagram[иҪ¬жҚўеӣҫ]
6
1
start

вҖў An FA is a program for classifying strings (return: accept, reject)
в—ҶIn other words, a program for recognizing a language
в—ҶFor a given string вҖҳxвҖҷ, if there is a transition sequence for вҖҳxвҖҷ to move from 
the start state to a certain accepting state, then we say вҖҳxвҖҷ is accepted by 
the FA. Otherwise, rejected
вҖў Language of FA = set of strings accepted by that FA
вҖў L(FA) вүЎ L(RE)
FA: Language
7

вҖў Are the following strings acceptable?
в—Ҷ0   вҲҡ
в—Ҷ1    Г—
в—Ҷ11110    вҲҡ
в—Ҷ11101    Г—
в—Ҷ11100    Г—
в—Ҷ1111110    вҲҡ
вҖў What language does the state graph recognize? вҲ‘ = {0, 1}
вҖў Any number of вҖҳ1вҖҷs followed by a single 0 
Example
8
1
start
0

вҖў Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA): the machine can exist in only 
one state at any given time[зЎ®е®ҡзҡ„жңүйҷҗзҠ¶жҖҒжңә]
в—ҶOne transition per input per state
в—ҶNo Оө-moves
в—ҶTakes only one path through the state graph
вҖў Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFA): the machine can exist in 
multiple states at the same time[йқһзЎ®е®ҡзҡ„жңүйҷҗзҠ¶жҖҒжңә]
в—ҶCan have multiple transitions for one input in a given state
в—ҶCan have Оө-moves
в—ҶCan choose which path to take
пҒ°An NFA accepts if some of these paths lead to accepting state at the end 
of input
DFA and NFA
9

вҖў 5 components  ( вҲ‘, S, n, F, Оҙ )
в—ҶAn input alphabet ОЈ 
в—ҶA set of states S
в—ҶA start state n вҲҲ S 
в—ҶA set of accepting states F вҠҶ S
в—ҶA set of transitions Оҙ: рқҗ’рқҗҡ
рқҗҲрқҗ§рқҗ©рқҗ®рқҗӯрқҗ’рқҗӣ

need one sequence of moves that lead to a final state

a final state and accept or the input string is rejected
 
 
вҲ’ Input string: aabb

вҖў FA can also be represented using transition table
вҖў Advantage
в—ҶWe can easily find the transitions on a given state and input. 
вҖў Disadvantage 
в—ҶIt takes a lot of space, when the input alphabet is large, yet most states do 
not have any moves on most of the input symbols.
пҒ°Need finite memory O(|S| * |вҲ‘|)

вҖў NFA and DFA are equivalent
From NFA to DFA
To show this we must prove every DFA can be converted into an 
NFA which accepts the same language, and vice-versa 
25

вҖў Theorem: L(NFA) вүЎ L(DFA)
в—ҶBoth recognize regular languages L(RE)
в—ҶеҜ№дәҺжҜҸдёӘNFA MеӯҳеңЁдёҖдёӘDFA M'пјҢдҪҝеҫ—L(M)=L(MвҖҷ)
пҒ°NFAдёҺNFAжҸҸиҝ°иғҪеҠӣзӣёеҗҢ!
вҖў Resulting DFA consumes more memory than NFA
в—ҶPotentially larger transition table as shown later
вҖў But DFAs are faster to execute
в—ҶFor DFAs, number of transitions == length of input
в—ҶFor NFAs, number of potential transitions can be larger
в—ҶNFAвҶ’ DFA conversion is needed because the speed of DFA far 
outweighs its extra memory consumption
From NFA to DFA
26

вҖў Recall DFA
в—ҶEvery state must have exactly one transition defined for every letter
в—ҶОө-moves are not allowed
пҒ°NFAs have multiple transition, while DFAs can only have one transition 
in one time
вҖў Subset construction[еӯҗйӣҶжһ„йҖ жі•]
в—ҶEach state of the constructed DFA corresponds to a set of NFA states
пҒ°After reading input рқ‘Һ1рқ‘Һ2 вҖҰ рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣ, the DFA is in that state which 
corresponds to the set of states that the NFA can reach, from its start 
state, following paths labeled рқ‘Һ1рқ‘Һ2 вҖҰ рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣ
From NFA to DFA
27

вҖў Two problem need to solve
в—ҶEliminate Оө-transition
в—ҶEliminate multiple transitions from a state on a single character
вҖў The Оө-closure of a set of states
в—ҶThe set of all states reachable by a series of zero or more Оө-transitions from 
the set of states
в—ҶThat is, about a set I
From NFA to DFA
28
Оө-closure(I) = IвҲӘ{s1, s2} 
s2
s1
Оө
Оө
I

Notion in the algorithm
вҖў Оө-closure(s)
The set of all states reachable by a series of 
zero or more Оө-transitions from state s
вҖў Оө-closure(T)
The set of all states reachable by a series 
of zero or more Оө-transitions from the set 
of states T
вҖў рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘Јрқ‘’рқ‘Ү, рқ‘Һ= рқ‘Ўрқ‘  вҲҲрқ‘Ү рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘ рқ‘ Хң
рқ‘Һрқ‘Ў}
Set of NFA states to which there is a 
transition on input symbol a from some 
state s in T
From NFA to DFA: Algorithm
29
Then, we will give a simple explanation 
by using the following symbols:
вҖў
I is a set of states,
вҖў

вҖў

рқ‘Һрқ‘Ў}
вҖў
рқҗјрқ‘Һ= Оө-closure(рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘Јрқ‘’рқҗј, рқ‘Һ)

вҖў Step1: Start by constructing Оө-closure of the start state
в—Ҷрқҗј = Оө-closure(state 0) = {0, 1, 3}
в—Ҷ{0, 1, 3} is a new state for DFA, marked T0

вҖў Step1: Start by constructing Оө-closure of the start state
в—Ҷрқҗј = Оө-closure(state 0) = {0, 1, 3}
вҖў Step2: Keep getting Оө-closure(move(рқҗј, x)) for each character x in ОЈ
в—ҶComputing for the new state until there are no more new states
в—Ҷрқҗј ={0, 1, 3}, move(рқҗј, a) = {2, 4}, Оө-closure({2, 4}) = {2, 4}
в—Ҷрқҗјрқ‘Һ = Оө-closure(move(рқҗј, a)) = {2, 4}
в—Ҷрқҗјрқ‘Ҹ = Оө-closure(move(рқҗј, b)) = {4}
в—Ҷ{2, 4} and {4} are sets of state that have never been obtained.

Converting DFAs to table-driven implementations

вҖў Theory: Given any DFA, there is an equivalent DFA containing a minimum 
number of states, and this minimum-state DFA is unique
вҖў Equivalent States
 If s and t are two states, they are equivalent if and only if:
в‘ s and t are both accepting states or both non-accepting states. 
в‘ЎFor each character xвҲҲОЈ, s and t have transitions on x to the equivalent states
Minimizing DFA 
36
States BC and AC do not 
need differentiation

вҖў The algorithm
Partitioning the states of a DFA into groups of states that cannot be 
distinguished (i.e., equivalent)
в‘ First, split the set of states into two sets, one consists of all accepting states 
and the other consists of all non-accepting states.
в‘ЎConsider the transitions on each character вҖҳxвҖҷ of the alphabet for each 
subset, and determine whether all the states in the subset are equivalent, 
or the subset should be split.
в‘ўContinue this process until no further splitting of sets occurs
Minimization Algorithm
37

{S, A, B} and {C, D, E, F},      {non-accepting states} and {accepting states}
в‘ЎConsider all states in each subset, check the transitions for each xвҲҲОЈ
Now we have two subsets {C,D,E,F} and {S,A,B}
For рқ‘°рқҹҸ = {C, D, E, F}

1. Initial: {0,3} and {1,2} 
2. Check all states in {1,2}
 state 2 have no transition, 
  state 1 have transition on b. 
 {1} and {2} are not equivalent.

 0 and 3 are equivalent states.
Result:  {0,3} {1} {2}.

вҖў NFA may be in many states at any time
вҖў How many different possible states in DFA?
в—ҶIf there are N states in NFA, the DFA must be in some subset of those N 
states
в—ҶHow many non-empty subsets are there?
пҒ°2рқ‘ҒвҲ’1
вҖў The resulting DFA has O(2N) space complexity, where N is 
number of original states in NFA
в—ҶFor real languages, the NFA and DFA have about same number of states
NFA вҶ’ DFA: Space Complexity[еӨҚжқӮеәҰ]
45

вҖў DFA execution
в—ҶRequires O(|X|) steps, where |X| is the input length
в—ҶEach step takes constant time
пҒ°If current state is S and input is c, then read T[S, c]
пҒ°Update current state to state T[S, c]
в—ҶTime complexity = O(|X|)
вҖў NFA execution
в—ҶRequires O(|X|) steps, where |X| is the input length
в—ҶEach step takes O(рқ‘Ғ2) time, where N is the number of states
пҒ°Current state is a set of potential states, up to N
пҒ°On input c, must union all T[Spotential, c], up to N times
- Each union operation takes O(N) time
в—ҶTime complexity = O(|X|* рқ‘Ғ2)
NFA вҶ’ DFA: Time Complexity[еӨҚжқӮеәҰ]
46
Deterministic: 
For input c, 
unique transition
Non-deterministic: 
For input c,
form current state, 
your can transit to any 
states (including itself)

of states T

accepting state

вҖў In general, find the longest match possible
в—ҶWe have seen examples
в—ҶOne more example: input string aabbb вҖҰ
пҒ°Have many prefixes that match the third pattern 
пҒ°Continue reading bвҖҷs until another a is met 
пҒ°Report the lexeme to be the initial aвҖҷs followed by as many bвҖҷs as there are
вҖў If same length, appearing first takes precedence[е…ҲеҮәзҺ°зҡ„дјҳе…Ҳ]
в—ҶString abb matches both the second and third pattern
в—ҶWe consider it as a lexeme for pattern2, since that pattern listed first

вҖў Example: to recognize the following tokens
в—Ҷ Identifiers: letter( letter | digit )*
в—Ҷ Keywords: if, then, else
вҖў Approach 1: make REs for keywords and place them before REs 
for identifiers so that they will take precedence
в—ҶWill result in a more bloated[иҮғиӮҝ] finite state machine
вҖў Approach 2: recognize keywords and identifiers using the same 
RE but differentiate using special keyword table 
в—ҶWill result in more streamlined finite state machine 
в—ҶBut extra table lookup is required
вҖў Usually approach 2 is more efficient than 1
How to Match Keywords?[еҢ№й…Қе…ій”®еӯ—]

вҖў L = {рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҹaрқ‘ӣ| n вүҘ 0} is not a Regular Language
в—ҶFA does not have any memory (FA cannot count)
пҒ°The above L requires to keep count of aвҖҷs before seeing bвҖҷs
вҖў Matching parenthesis is not a RL[жӢ¬еҸ·еҢ№й…ҚдёҚжҳҜжӯЈеҲҷиҜӯиЁҖ]
вҖў Any language with nested structure is not a RL
if вҖҰ if вҖҰ else вҖҰ else
вҖў Regular Languages
в—ҶWeakest formal languages that are widely used [жңҖејұзҡ„еҪўејҸиҜӯиЁҖ]
вҖў We need a more powerful formalism
The Limits of Regular Languages
69

вҖў Regular languages are expressive enough for tokens
в—ҶCan express identifiers, strings, comments, etc.
вҖў However, it is the weakest (least expressive) language
в—ҶMany languages are not regular
в—ҶC programming language is not
пҒ°The language matching braces вҖң{{{...}}}вҖқ is also not
в—ҶFA does not have any memory (FA cannot count)
пҒ°L = {рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ӣ| n вүҘ 1}
пҒ°Crucial for analyzing languages with nested structures[еөҢеҘ—з»“жһ„] (e.g. 
nested for loop in C language)
вҖў We need a more powerful language for parsing
в—ҶLater, we will discuss context-free languages (CFGs)
